attributes.stem:
A 47-year-old woman presents with a three day history of muscle ache, diarrhoea and headache. During the same time she has had a high fever and pain behind her eyes. She was previously in good health and returned from a holiday in the Philippines one week ago. She was on antimalarial prophylaxis during the trip.

On examination her blood pressure is 118/84 mmHg, pulse 112/min and temperature 39°C. She looks unwell and there is a maculopapular rash over her trunk and limbs. Petechiae are visible over her mucous membranes. There are no signs of nuchal rigidity or photophobia.

Her laboratory investigations show:

  • Hb: 136 g/L (120-170)

  • WCC: 2.7 × 109/L (4.0–11.0)

  • PLT: 91 × 109/L (150–450)

  • ALT: 108 U/L (5 - 35)

  • AST: 99 U/L (5 – 35)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient is most likely suffering from dengue fever, which is a febrile illness transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquitoes. The virus is endemic in countries in the Southeast Asian and Western Pacific regions, the Americas, Africa, and the Eastern Mediterranean. Although dengue is not endemic in Australia, periodic outbreaks can occur, especially in the northeastern corner.

The characteristic features of dengue include the abrupt onset of high fever, myalgia/arthralgia, and a diffuse skin rash. Gastrointestinal symptoms such as nausea and vomiting, diarrhoea and anorexia are common. Clinicians should be vigilant for signs of haemorrhagic manifestations, such as skin or mucosal bleeding, as these may indicate severe plasma leakage and progression to shock. Leukopenia and thrombocytopenia are frequently observed in dengue infections, while liver enzymes are often elevated.

Close differentials to dengue virus infection include Chikungunya and Zika virus infection, all of which are transmitted by mosquitoes. Severe peripheral arthralgia or arthritis is a prominent feature of Chikungunya infection, to the extent that some patients avoid mobilisation. The presence of a bleeding tendency makes dengue a more probable diagnosis.

Chagas disease is caused by infection with the protozoan parasite Trypanosoma cruzi and is endemic in Latin America only. Most patients with acute infection exhibit mild, nonspecific symptoms, such as malaise, fever, and anorexia. Chronic infection can develop, potentially leading to Chagas cardiomyopathy.

Malaria is considered a less likely diagnosis in this case due to the patient being on antimalarial prophylaxis, and the presence of a rash, which is uncommon in malarial infections.

Bacterial meningitis is unlikely as there are no signs of meningism.
learningPoints: Dengue fever is a mosquito-borne illness that manifests with sudden high fever, myalgia/arthralgia, and a diffuse skin rash.
references: Muller DA, Depelsenaire AC, Young PR. Clinical and laboratory diagnosis of dengue virus infection. J Infect Dis [Internet]. 2017 Mar 1 [cited 2023 Jul 1];215(suppl_2):S89-95. Available from: https://academic.oup.com/jid/article/215/suppl_2/S89/3574518 doi: 10.1093/infdis/jiw649
